Title: Pas 'not disappointed' despite Johor whitewash, says Hadi
Article:
SEREMBAN: Pas is not disappointed over its failure to win any seats in the Johor state election as the state remains under Malay-Muslim leadership, party president Tan Sri Abdul Hadi Awang said.
He said Pas was willing to make sacrifices by directing its members to support Barisan Nasional (BN) candidates in areas where the party did not contest to ensure Malay-Muslim political power was maintained.
"We are grateful to Allah that Johor is safe. It does not matter that we did not win a single seat because we are pursuing reforms (islah).
"Pas was willing to sacrifice by directing members to support BN candidates in areas we did not contest, including MCA candidates," he said at the Pas Negeri Sembilan Perdana Dinner in Paroi last night.
Hadi said the move ensured Pakatan Harapan (PH) was defeated, paving the way for BN's landslide victory.
However, the Marang member of parliament reminded BN not to become complacent, saying the coalition would not remain strong forever.
"Umno must not think it will be strong forever. In the last election, there were constituencies where Umno lost and Malay DAP candidates won," he said.
He added that Pas contested the Johor state election to preserve Malay leadership, rather than to lead the state government.
"Pas cannot take over Johor because Umno has already won there. However, if too many Malay parties contest, it will cause the Malays to split," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hadi says Pas-Umno seat negotiations ongoing for Negri polls
Article:
SEREMBAN: Pas is prepared to accept an Umno leader as the Negri Sembilan menteri besar candidate if cooperation between the two parties is finalised for the Aug 1 state election.
Pas president Tan Sri Abdul Hadi Awang said seat negotiations between both sides were ongoing.
"Yes, we are working towards cooperation (with Umno and Barisan Nasional). The discussions have been very positive.
"We accept the reality that Umno dominates Negri Sembilan, so they will lead (as menteri besar)," he told a press conference after the Negri Sembilan Pas Premier Dinner in Paroi last night.
Hadi said the discussions included Umno retaining its 14 seats, while Pas would keep its three existing seats.
The two seats previously held by Bersatu will be allocated to either Pas or Umno.
"Meanwhile, the 17 seats currently held by Pakatan Harapan (PH) will be distributed among the cooperating parties, including Umno, Pas, MCA, MIC, and Gerakan.
"So far, negotiations are progressing smoothly without issues. What is important is that Malays and Muslims must lead Negri Sembilan, just like in Johor," he said.
Abdul Hadi added that Pas had yet to decide whether to contest under the Perikatan Nasional banner or its own party logo.
"We are still deliberating. The final decision will be announced before nomination day on Thursday night," he said.
Earlier in his speech, he stressed that Pas would adopt a different strategy for the Negri Sembilan polls compared to the Johor state election to win over voters and secure as many seats as possible.
In the 15th state election in 2023, PH formed the state government alongside BN after winning 31 out of 36 state seats, with PH taking 17 seats, BN 14, and PN five.
However, for the upcoming election, PH and BN are moving independently, with both coalitions contesting all 36 state seats.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Typhoon Bavi batters eastern China, threatens days of heavy rain
Article:
WENZHOU: Typhoon Bavi, the most powerful storm to strike mainland China this year, brought heavy rain to the eastern coast on Sunday and lashed densely populated cities with violent winds, testing the country's ability to cope with extreme weather.
Bavi had weakened by Sunday morning to a tropical storm as it pushed inland, but forecasters warned that the France-sized storm system could unleash prolonged and widespread rain across eastern and northern China in the coming days.
Over 2.8 million people have been evacuated, according to a Reuters tally of figures reported by state media.
Of those, more than 2.2 million were in Zhejiang, an economic and technological powerhouse in the world's second-largest economy. There has been no official reports of deaths or injuries.
Bavi struck Zhejiang's coastal city of Yuhuan at around 11.20pm (1520 GMT) on Saturday before making a second landfall in Yueqing, part of the city of Wenzhou, at around midnight.
"The winds were very strong," Yueqing resident Li Liangxing told Reuters. "We could hear roof tiles and tree branches falling. Of course we were scared, but we live by the sea, so we're used to it."
Gesturing toward a flooded canal beside his residential compound, Li said he had never seen the water so high.
"There used to be a walkway there, but now it's underwater."
More than 1,300 trees fell across Yueqing, with more than 700 of them uprooted entirely, state broadcaster CCTV reported. The deepest flooding reached roughly half the height of a vehicle tire.
Emergency crews on Sunday deployed excavators and chainsaws to clear waterlogged streets littered with fallen trees.
In the city's mountainous north, footage aired by CCTV showed a landslide that sent large boulders tumbling onto a mountain road, while swollen river waters submerged nearby trees.
In Kanmen, a coastal fishing town in Yuhuan, 72-year-old parcel shop owner Lin Yongjin was counting the cost of Bavi.
His shop, which faces the sea, bore the brunt of the storm. Metal frames supporting the entrance canopy had collapsed, and a window in a neighbouring building had been blown out. Lin estimated the typhoon had caused more than 6,000 yuan (US$885) in damage.
"After it came ashore, there was nothing we could do. Rainwater poured into the house. We spent the whole night dealing with it and didn't get to sleep until after 5am," he said.
Having lived through many typhoons, Lin said this one stood out. "It was a very powerful typhoon. It made landfall right here in Kanmen. We were right in its path."
FLIGHTS, TRAINS DISRUPTED
Bavi passed northern Taiwan on Saturday, bringing strong winds and driving rain across much of the island. The storm dumped nearly 80cm of rain in one area in the northern county of Miaoli.
Taiwan's fire department said on Sunday that 134 people had been injured, mainly due to falling off motorbikes, slipping or being struck by objects. It reported no deaths. The transport ministry said 137 international flights had been cancelled on Sunday, along with 62 domestic trips.
The disruption also extended to China's transport networks. In Zhejiang's provincial capital, Hangzhou, two major train stations suspended all services and 327 flights were cancelled at Xiaoshan International Airport.
In neighbouring Shanghai, a total of 1,620 train trips and 684 flights were cancelled, state-backed The Paper reported.
By Sunday afternoon, Bavi had moved into eastern Anhui province and was forecast to turn northeast before entering the northern Yellow Sea on Tuesday, China's National Meteorological Centre said.
The storm is expected to dump heavy to torrential rain across the provinces of Jilin, Liaoning, Hebei, Shandong, Jiangsu and Anhui from Monday, exacerbating flood risks in areas that have already been soaked by earlier downpours.
In Hebei province's Qianxi county, where nearly 190mm of rain fell from Saturday to Sunday morning. Social media footage verified by Reuters showed rescuers using an inflatable boat to reach people stranded atop a partially submerged car after floodwaters transformed a public square into a muddy lake.
Even as Bavi weakened after landfall, its large circulation can continue to generate destructive weather hundreds of kilometres inland, said Benjamin Horton, the dean of the School of Energy and Environment at the City University of Hong Kong.
Scientists have warned China could face more extreme weather this year with the expected emergence of the El Nino weather pattern, which can drive up temperatures and shift typhoon tracks westward toward the country's coast.
"Rapid intensification (of typhoons) reduces preparation time for communities and emergency managers, making these events particularly challenging," Horton said. -REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil jumps three pct after US, Iran escalate strikes in Mideast
Article:
Oil prices jumped on Monday as Iran expanded strikes on Gulf states following attacks by the United States, threatening energy shipments via the Strait of Hormuz.
Brent crude futures climbed US$2.34, or 3.08 per cent, to US$78.35 by 2311 GMT, while US West Texas Intermediate crude rose US$2.21, or 3.09 per cent, to US$73.62 a barrel.
Over the weekend, Tehran extended strikes on Qatar and the United Arab Emirates while the US launched further strikes on Iran, the latest in a cycle of attacks and counter-attacks over shipping through the strait.
US President Donald Trump said on Sunday that the Strait of Hormuz is open to commercial traffic, although Iran declared earlier that it closed the strait after a vessel traveled on an unapproved route and was struck.
Six vessels transited the strait on Sunday, shiptracking data from Kpler showed, the lowest number in five weeks.
The escalating attacks cast further doubt on the future of an interim US-Iranian agreement signed last month that aimed to reopen the strait and end the war after a further 60 days of negotiations.
Following the agreement, global oil supply rose by 4.1 million barrels per day in June, but remained 9.4 million bpd below pre-war levels, the International Energy Agency said in its monthly report on Friday.
"Hopes of a relatively quick resolution to the recent skirmishes may be in doubt after tension escalated over the weekend," ANZ analysts said in a note.
IG market analyst Tony Sycamore said the relatively tame rise in oil prices suggested the market was taking the view that the current flare up represented an escalation within a fragile truce and fell well short of a complete collapse of the ceasefire.
"How accurate that view is remains to be seen," he said in a note.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Pray for us,' says Bella ahead of Syed Saddiq appeal ruling [WATCH]
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Actress and singer Bella Astillah said she was filled with emotion ahead of the Federal Court's decision on the prosecution's appeal against Muar MP Syed Saddiq Syed Abdul Rahman's acquittal on four corruption-related charges.
Asked how she was feeling, Bella, who is engaged to Syed Saddiq, said: "InsyaAllah, please pray for us."
Bella was among the first to arrive at the Palace of Justice, joining Syed Saddiq's family as they awaited the court's decision.
Syed Saddiq has yet to arrive.
Deputy public prosecutor Datuk Wan Shaharuddin Wan Ladin said briefly: "I hope for the best for both parties."
More than 20 supporters were already inside the building ahead of the 9am proceedings after undergoing security screening at the entrance.
They were directed to a designated waiting area, where the proceedings would be broadcast live.
The apex court is set to
decide the prosecution's appeal
to reinstate Syed Saddiq's conviction and sentence on four corruption-related offences linked to Bersatu Youth funds. funds.
The ruling comes two weeks after the court postponed its decision, originally scheduled for June 30, due to the medical leave of judge Datuk Che Ruzima Ghazali.
Court of Appeal president Datuk Seri Abu Bakar Jais, who chairs the three-member bench, had announced that the decision would instead
be delivered today
. The other member of the bench is Datuk Collin Lawrence Sequerah.
If the appeal is allowed, Syed Saddiq, 34, faces up to seven years' imprisonment, two strokes of the rotan and a RM10 million fine. He is represented by senior lawyer Datuk Hisyam Teh Poh Teik.
The prosecution is seeking to reinstate the High Court's conviction on charges of criminal breach of trust (CBT), dishonest misappropriation of property and money laundering involving Bersatu Youth funds.
The High Court had sentenced Syed Saddiq to three years' jail and one stroke of the rotan for abetting CBT involving RM1 million in Armada funds, and two years' jail and another stroke of the rotan for misappropriating RM120,000 from Armada's Maybank Islamic account.
For two money laundering charges, he was sentenced to two years' jail and fined RM10 million, with all custodial sentences ordered to run concurrently.
The Court of Appeal later acquitted the former youth and sports minister, prompting the prosecution's final appeal to the Federal Court.
Syed Saddiq was accused of abetting Bersatu Youth assistant treasurer Rafiq Hakim Razali in committing CBT involving RM1 million at CIMB Bank, KL Sentral, on March 6, 2020.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tributes paid to US senator and Trump ally Lindsey Graham
Article:
WASHINGTON: US Senator Lindsey Graham, a South Carolina Republican and key Trump ally who was elected to the Senate four times, died aged 71 following a "brief and sudden illness," his office said Sunday.
Graham made a name for himself with his work on foreign policy and in recent years urged both the Trump and Biden administrations to back Kyiv's fight against Russia's invasion. He was a staunch supporter of the Iran war.
Here are some of the reactions to Graham's sudden passing:
- US PRESIDENT DONALD TRUMP -
US President Donald Trump praised the influential senator on Sunday, calling him "one of the greatest people" in a post on his Truth Social site.
"Senator Lindsey Graham, one of the greatest people and Senators I have ever known, is dead! He was always working, and was a true American Patriot. Lindsey will be greatly missed!!!"
- FORMER PRESIDENT JOE BIDEN -
Former president Joe Biden said he and his wife were shocked by Graham's sudden death.
"Lindsey and I served together in Congress for over a decade, and worked closely on many issues throughout the years. We traveled the world together as members of the Senate Foreign Relations committee.
"We disagreed often, and sometimes loudly. Lindsey and I did agree on the profound importance of public service. Like me, he loved the Senate as an institution, even with all its flaws and complexities."
- FORMER PRESIDENT GEORGE W. BUSH -
Former president George W. Bush called Graham a "knowledgeable Senator who understood how the world works and how important America's international engagement is to resist tyranny.
"He was a kind and funny man who loved our country and loved serving it. South Carolina was fortunate to have such a committed public servant in its corner. And I was fortunate to have known Lindsey Graham."
- ISRAELI PRIME MINISTER BENJAMIN NETANYAHU -
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu called Graham a "great friend of Israel."
"In our recent meeting, I said, 'Lindsey is a great friend of Israel and a cherished friend of mine. We have no better friend than Lindsey.' Lindsey understood that the security of Israel and America are inseparable," he said in a statement released by his office.
"He devoted his life to defending America, strengthening our alliance and standing up for the free world. Israel has lost one of its greatest friends."
- UKRAINIAN PRESIDENT VOLODYMYR ZELENSKYY -
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy also said he was deeply saddened by the death of Graham, whom he met in Kyiv as recently as Friday.
"He visited Ukraine ten times during the years of Russia's full-scale invasion and was here with our people when it was most needed...  America and the world have lost a determined leader," Zelenskyy said in a Facebook post.
- EU CHIEF URSULA VON DER LEYEN -
European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen applauded Graham's fight to support Ukraine, calling him a "determined and fearless leader."
"Senator Graham fought until the very end to support Ukraine's fight for freedom and raise the cost of Russia's war of aggression," she said in a post on X.
"He worked tirelessly to strengthen sanctions, in close coordination with the EU. A determined and fearless leader. He will be deeply missed."
- NATO CHIEF MARK RUTTE -
"So sad to learn of the sudden passing of my friend Lindsey Graham," Rutte posted online.
"He was a powerful advocate for America who believed strongly in the Nato alliance and was actively working to bring an end to Russia's war against Ukraine."
- ISRAELI PRESIDENT ISAAC HERZOG -
Israeli President Isaac Herzog said the news left him "shocked and heartbroken."
"Senator Graham was a beacon of moral clarity and a true leader of the U.S.-Israel partnership," he said in a post on X.
- TAIWAN PRESIDENT LAI CHING-TE -
Taiwan President Lai Ching-te said he was "deeply saddened by the sudden passing."
"A true friend of Taiwan, Senator Graham will always be remembered as an unwavering advocate & defender of freedom."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Vietnam arrests speedboat captain after deadly capsize
Article:
HANOI: Vietnamese police arrested on Sunday the captain of a speedboat that capsized near southern Phu Quoc island, killing 15 Indian tourists, authorities said.
The incident occurred on Saturday as the boat carrying 32 Indian tourists, three Vietnamese crew members and a tour guide overturned in rough seas off May Rut Ngoai islet, state media said.
Sailors and rescuers recovered 21 people, Tuoi Tre newspaper reported, but 15 passengers died.
Police in southern An Giang province said they arrested the 57-year-old captain of the boat for "violations of laws" resulting in the capsize.
Tuoi Tre reported that "initial assessments by Vietnamese authorities suggest this was an unavoidable incident caused by extreme weather conditions" that shifted rapidly.
State media quoted sailors involved in the rescue effort as saying there were high waves but no rain at the time, and several other tourist boats were operating in the area.
One witness, Ha Van Loc, said he spotted the overturned vessel with about a dozen men clinging to its hull.
Several people were drifting at sea and not wearing life jackets, Loc said, according to state outlet VNExpress.
Loc and his crew threw lifebuoys with ropes to pull them aboard his boat and called for additional help, with jet skis used to transport victims ashore.
At least 15 survivors have been discharged from hospital and were travelling back to India, New Delhi's embassy in Hanoi said on Sunday, adding that one person in critical condition was receiving treatment at a hospital in Phu Quoc.
The bodies of the 15 deceased would be transported to India, the embassy said.
Located just south of Vietnam's largest island Phu Quoc, May Rut Ngoai is known for its pristine blue waters, white sand beaches and accessible diving reefs.
Visitors often access the islet and others nearby via speedboat from the main island.
State media reports on Sunday said several tourism companies suspended their speedboat services between the islets after the deadly capsize.
Phu Quoc has seen a major tourism boom in recent years, with more than 1.8 million foreigners visiting the island last year.
Boating accidents are a regular occurrence in the Southeast Asian nation, where extreme weather, frequent flooding and inadequate maintenance have led to tragedies.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Recurring income anchors Tropicana's growth strategy
Article:
PETALING JAYA: TROPICANA Corp Bhd, which posted a 20.5 per cent increase in revenue to RM313.7 million for the first quarter ended March 31, 2026 (Q1 FY26), from RM260.4 million a year earlier, is laying the groundwork for a more resilient earnings profile by expanding its recurring income base.
The shift will be driven by its partnership with global hospitality operator Accor Group, marking a strategic move beyond its traditional property development business and creating a new stream of recurring revenue.
Group chief executive officer Dennis Ng said the company is strengthening its recurring income portfolio through an asset-light business model, broadening its presence beyond township development into hospitality and other complementary businesses.
A key step in that strategy is its collaboration with Accor to launch Malaysia's first branded Accor condotel, Mercure Living Genting Highlands, which Ng described as an important milestone in diversifying the group's earnings base.
"The partnership reflects Tropicana's broader strategy to build sustainable recurring income streams that complement its core township development business.
"Our core business remains property development, but we are evolving our business model to create a stronger and more balanced platform for long-term growth," he said.
Ng said strategic partnerships allow Tropicana to extract greater value from its developments by enhancing project positioning, strengthening buyer confidence and creating professionally managed hospitality assets capable of generating long-term recurring income.
Operationally, Tropicana's cost optimisation initiatives continued to deliver results, while unbilled sales remained strong at RM1.7 billion as at end‑March, underpinning its earnings visibility.
The group also continued to strengthen its balance sheet through disciplined debt management.
In March, Tropicana completed the full redemption of its remaining RM89.43 million Tranche 1 perpetual sukuk. This was followed by the redemption of RM133.2 million under Tranche 5 of its RM1.5 billion IMTN 2020 Sukuk Wakalah Programme in April.
The group had earlier redeemed RM139 million under Tranche 4 in October 2025 before successfully upsizing and issuing RM300 million under its IMTN 2024 Sukuk Wakalah Programme the following month, supported by strong investor demand.
Reflecting the group's deleveraging progress and landbank optimisation efforts, MARC Ratings recently revised Tropicana's outlook to "positive" from "stable" while affirming its "A" rating.
Looking ahead, management said Tropicana will continue pivoting towards higher‑growth segments such as industrial and logistics developments, renewable energy and data centre‑related projects while maintaining its core township and mixed‑use developments across its 545.5 ha landbank with an estimated gross development value of RM102.6 billion.
"As market expectations continue to evolve, we must also evolve. Through strategic partnerships, we can unlock greater value from our developments, strengthen buyer confidence and create professionally managed assets that generate sustainable recurring income," Ng said.
He said Tropicana will continue leveraging strategic partnerships, hospitality and other complementary businesses to unlock greater value from its sizeable landbank while building a diversified, future-ready real estate platform that delivers sustainable long-term value for customers, investors and shareholders.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tropicana drives growth through Accor tie-up
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: TROPICANA Corp Bhd is accelerating its transformation into a diversified real estate platform by expanding beyond its traditional township development business into hospitality, recurring income, asset management and other complementary businesses to strengthen earnings resilience and unlock greater long-term value from its strategic landbank.
The property developer's partnership with global hospitality group Accor SA to launch Malaysia's first branded Accor condotel, Mercure Living Genting Highlands, represents a key milestone in this strategy.
Through an asset-light partnership model, the collaboration enables Tropicana to unlock greater value from its landbank while integrating international hospitality expertise into its developments and creating sustainable recurring income streams.
The collaboration, through a hotel management agreement, will integrate Accor's hospitality expertise into selected Tropicana developments, starting with Tropicana Grandhill, a 45.33ha wellness-focused precinct within the 241.19ha Tropicana WindCity integrated township in Genting Highlands. The partnership strengthens project positioning, enhances buyer confidence and creates professionally managed hospitality assets that generate recurring income.
Tropicana group chief executive officer Dennis Ng said the company's core business remains property development, but the company is increasingly leveraging strategic partnerships to create long-term value rather than owning and operating assets entirely on its own.
"We are moving into hospitality by partnering with Accor to enhance the value of our developments through an internationally recognised hotel operator," Ng told 'Business Times'.
"Our primary focus continues to be real estate development. What is changing is how we unlock and extract greater value from our developments through strategic partnerships," he said.
Ng said Tropicana is adopting an asset-light strategy by working with partners that contribute funding and operational expertise, allowing the company to monetise its landbank while reducing capital intensity.
"The objective is to create recurring income, strengthen our balance sheet and establish a sustainable business model that benefits all stakeholders," he said.
He added that the approach would enable Tropicana to recycle capital from selected assets into new growth opportunities while reducing the burden of owning and operating the assets directly.
"As we scale forward, we see that the model can be replicated, especially across our developments in Langkawi, Genting Highlands and Johor.
"These are the three key locations where we can further integrate hospitality and value-added services to strengthen our recurring income model and support long-term sustainable growth," Ng said.
GLOBAL NETWORK ENHANCES INVESTMENT APPEAL
Under the Mercure Living Genting Highlands model, property owners will gain access to Accor's global reservation network, the ALL–Accor Live Limitless loyalty programme, professionally managed rental and leasing services, and internationally recognised hospitality standards.
Owners will also benefit from potential rental income, global marketing exposure and operational support from Accor, while enjoying lifestyle privileges such as preferential room rates, member rewards and access to Accor's global portfolio of hotels and resorts, enhancing both personal use and long-term asset value.
The initiative forms part of Tropicana's broader strategy to develop integrated communities that combine residential, commercial, hospitality, leisure and lifestyle offerings under its live-work-play-learn-shop-stay township concept.
As consumer preferences evolve and the real estate sector becomes increasingly experience-driven, Tropicana believes future growth will be supported not only by property sales but also by recurring income generated through strategic partnerships in hospitality operations, rental management, lifestyle services, township activation and strategic asset management initiatives.
Ng said Tropicana was evolving its business model to create a stronger and more balanced platform for long-term growth.
"For more than four decades, Tropicana has established itself as one of Malaysia's leading township developers. As the market continues to evolve, we are equally evolving our business model to create a stronger and more balanced platform for long-term growth.
"Our objective is to complement our traditional development business with sustainable recurring income streams that enhance shareholder value while strengthening the competitiveness and resilience of our townships," he said.
Ng said the collaboration with Accor demonstrates how global hospitality partnerships can unlock new value from the company's developments and create professionally managed lifestyle assets that benefit homeowners, investors and the wider community.
Tropicana managing director for marketing, sales and business development Ixora Ang said homeowners would enjoy fully serviced condotel living and automatic Gold membership under the ALL–Accor Live Limitless loyalty programme.
"Join more than 100 million ALL members and enjoy Gold member privileges across over 4,500 Accor hotels representing 45 brands worldwide. On top of this, homeowners will have peace of mind knowing that branded residences offer stronger long-term investment potential," she said.
Scheduled for completion in 2028, the RM1.06 billion gross development value project will comprise 1,443 fully furnished units with kitchen and dining facilities alongside amenities, including an all-day dining restaurant, executive lounge, multipurpose function hall, heated swimming pool, fitness centre and other lifestyle facilities.
Ang said the development has achieved an encouraging 88 per cent take-up rate.
"Branded residences continue to attract strong interest globally due to their premium positioning, professional management and investment appeal. We believe this partnership will further strengthen buyer confidence and enhance the long-term value proposition of Tropicana WindCity," she said.
Within this ecosystem, Mercure Living Genting Highlands plays a key role in the premium-midscale segment, combining the flexibility of individually owned units with the operational excellence of a global hospitality platform.
The new condotel concept offers investors attractive rental opportunities through professionally managed programmes while leveraging Accor's international distribution network, ALL loyalty programme and global brand standards to maximise occupancy, enhance guest experiences and strengthen long-term asset value.
More importantly, this hospitality-led positioning elevates the lifestyle and investment proposition of Tropicana WindCity by introducing the world's largest Mercure Living development, featuring a professionally managed extended-stay accommodation concept that further enriches Genting Highlands' evolving hospitality landscape.
Mercure Living Genting Highlands focuses on extended-stay and serviced residence formats tailored for modern travellers seeking longer, more flexible stays. It blends hotel services with residential comfort, appealing to business and lifestyle segments.
It also provides scalable, lower-risk entry points into branded hospitality real estate while benefiting from Accor's global demand engine, operational expertise and proven ability to enhance asset performance.
Accor Asia chief operating officer for the premium, midscale and economy divisions Garth Simmons said Mercure Living Genting Highlands would expand the group's extended-stay hospitality offering in Malaysia.
"As travellers increasingly seek accommodation that combines flexibility, comfort and authentic local experiences, we believe this development will be well-positioned to meet those needs while contributing to the continued growth of Genting Highlands as a leisure destination," he said.
Accor Asia chief development officer Andrew Langdon said Genting Highlands had strengthened its position as a major tourism destination, with growing demand from domestic and international travellers.
Speaking at the signing ceremony, he said Mercure Living Genting Highlands, which will be the world's largest Mercure Living development, is positioned to set a new benchmark for branded extended-stay hospitality in Malaysia.
"With visitor arrivals surpassing those of renowned destinations such as Bali and Phuket, we are confident that demand from international and domestic travellers will continue to grow.
"Mercure Living Genting Highlands is strategically positioned to meet this demand as the world's largest Mercure Living development. It will set a new benchmark for branded extended-stay hospitality in Malaysia," he said.
Expanding beyond property with hospitality, digital push
Tropicana believes this integrated model has the potential to be replicated across selected developments within its nationwide portfolio, particularly in tourism-led, lifestyle, waterfront and highland destinations where branded hospitality can complement residential, commercial and leisure offerings.
Among the locations identified for future opportunities are Tropicana WindCity in Genting Highlands, Lido Waterfront Boulevard in Johor, Tropicana Cenang and selected future coastal and resort-oriented developments across Genting Highlands, Johor and Langkawi, where hospitality and destination placemaking can further enhance long-term value.
Beyond hospitality, Tropicana continues to diversify into complementary sectors that support its long-term growth strategy, including digital infrastructure and other recurring income platforms that leverage the group's sizeable strategic landbank and integrated township expertise.
Supporting the company's strategic evolution is Tropicana's ongoing digital transformation journey through platforms such as T360 and T Journey, which are designed to enhance the customer experience beyond property ownership.
Serving as the company's integrated digital platform, T360 combines smart property management, lifestyle content and the Tropicana Privilege loyalty programme within a seamless digital ecosystem.
Property owners can conveniently access property information, manage maintenance and billing, submit and track defect rectification, book facilities and amenities, receive community updates, and enjoy a connected digital experience throughout their entire homeownership lifecycle.
Beyond property management, T360 also features curated lifestyle content through 'T Magazine' and the Spill the T podcast, alongside exclusive rewards, promotions and privileges offered through its growing network of merchant partners.
T Journey extends Tropicana's hospitality and property management capabilities by providing professionally managed rental services for selected developments, beginning with its Langkawi projects, while offering curated travel, accommodation, dining and lifestyle experiences through strategic partnerships.
Together, T360 and T Journey strengthen customer engagement, enhance homeowner convenience and loyalty, and create additional service-based revenue opportunities.
Tropicana said these platforms form an integral part of its holistic growth strategy, supporting its long-term vision of building connected, future-ready communities while creating sustainable value for customers, partners and shareholders.
The company believes its diversification strategy will progressively strengthen earnings resilience by reducing reliance on conventional property development while creating sustainable long-term value for customers, partners, investors and shareholders.
Moving forward, Tropicana plans to continue exploring strategic collaborations with leading global and regional partners that complement its integrated township expertise and reinforce its position as one of Malaysia's most progressive community developers.
As the company expands its hospitality platform alongside its township portfolio, these initiatives are expected to support Tropicana's long-term objective of building a more diversified, future-ready real estate platform capable of delivering sustainable long-term value for customers, partners, investors and shareholders alike.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: SD Property's UniRazak project seen with upside from higher plot ratio
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Sime Darby Property Bhd's proposed RM900 million redevelopment of the Universiti Tun Abdul Razak (UniRazak) site in Kuala Lumpur could deliver further upside if it secures a higher development intensity under the Kuala Lumpur Local Plan 2040.
Hong Leong Investment Bank Bhd (HLIB) views the acquisition positively, noting that the estimated gross development value (GDV) of RM900 million is based on an assumed plot ratio of eight times.
"However, under the Kuala Lumpur Local Plan 2040, commercial land within the city-centre area may be eligible for a plot ratio of up to 10 times, subject to approval, which could provide further upside to the project's GDV," Tan said.
SD Property recently announced the RM160 million acquisition of Wisma UniRazak, a 15-storey office building on a 1.46-acre freehold site along Jalan Tun Razak, which it plans to redevelop into a premium serviced apartment project with supporting commercial components.
Subject to planning approvals, the project is expected to be launched in 2028 after the existing tenancies expire by Oct 31, 2027, with completion targeted within five years.
HLIB views the acquisition positively, as the implied land cost of RM252 per square foot per plot ratio (psf ppr) and land cost-to-GDV ratio of 17.8 per cent are slightly below recent nearby transactions by Mah Sing Group Bhd and Paramount Corp Bhd.
The site also benefits from strong connectivity via the Ampang Park LRT/MRT interchange, said HLIB analyst Tan Kai Shuen.
"For comparison, Paramount's recent acquisition of the nearby Jalan U-Thant land in March 2026 was at a higher land cost-to-GDV ratio of 23.4 per cent. That said, SD Property will incur additional costs to demolish the existing Unirazak building," he said in a note.
HLIB noted that recent strategic land transactions in the Klang Valley have been concluded at between RM267 psf ppr and RM500 psf ppr.
Mah Sing acquired the Corus Hotel site in August 2025 at RM287 psf ppr, while Paramount purchased a Jalan U-Thant site at RM267 psf ppr in March 2026. More recently, EXSIM acquired the Angkasa Raya development rights at RM500 psf ppr.
At RM314 psf ppr, SD Property's acquisition sits above the Mah Sing and Paramount transactions but remains well below the Angkasa Raya benchmark.
"However, if SD Property is able to lift its plot ratio to 10 times, its entry cost would decline to RM252 psf ppr, falling slightly below Mah Sing's and Paramount's acquisition costs," Tan said.
He also highlighted the site's strong locational advantages, given its proximity to the Ampang Park LRT and MRT interchange, providing direct connectivity to the KLCC, Tun Razak Exchange, and other major commercial hubs.
"We gather that SD Property is targeting an average selling price of slightly above RM2,000 psf for the project, broadly similar to the targeted pricing for Mah Sing's Corus Hotel redevelopment," Tan said, adding that the project pricing is above the U-Thant area benchmark of around RM1,300 psf and the listed resale prices of nearby residential projects at approximately RM1,600 psf.
He said that in terms of location, Mah Sing's Corus Hotel site has the advantage of being within walking distance to KLCC, with some units enjoying the KLCC view.
"SD Property has demonstrated its ability to deliver premium residential products through its KLGCC projects, including Jendela Residences and The Ophera. If SDP is able to replicate this success by delivering a differentiated product with strong design, amenities and branding, we believe the project could still command robust demand despite the higher price point," HLIB said.
HLIB maintained its earnings forecasts and reiterated its "Buy" recommendation on Sime Darby Property with an unchanged target price of RM2.10.
"We continue to view the stock as a multi-year growth story, anchored by its exposure to the thriving industrial segment and the resilient residential landed demand in the Greater Klang Valley. Furthermore, earnings visibility is further supported by the scaling of recurring income streams from its investment property portfolio."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tariff hike to lift Westports earnings
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Higher container tariffs are expected to more than offset rising fuel costs at Westports Holdings Bhd, supporting stronger earnings in the second quarter of financial year 2026 (Q2 FY26), according to RHB Research.
The firm has maintained its "Buy" call on the port operator and raised its discounted cash flow-based target price to RM7.49 from RM6.55 after increasing its tariff assumptions.
Westport is scheduled to announce its second quarter results on July 24.
RHB Research expects core profit after tax and minority interests (Patami) to come in between RM280 million and RM320 million, representing year-on-year growth of about 20 per cent to 40 per cent.
The firm said the stronger earnings outlook would be supported by higher container handling charges and continued growth in container throughput.
RHB Research also said its forecasts had already factored in a 30 per cent increase in fuel costs compared with pre-conflict levels, limiting the impact of any further rise in fuel prices following renewed geopolitical tensions involving the US and Iran.
It added that fuel consumption is expected to decline by about 10 per cent in the fourth quarter after Westports deploys 60 electric trucks by the third quarter.
The company is also exempt from the automatic fuel adjustment mechanism, shielding it from higher electricity costs linked to fuel price movements.
On the demand side, RHB Research said Malaysia's strong export performance, particularly in the electrical and electronics sector, should continue to support container throughput.
It noted that exports rose 24.3 per cent in the first five months of 2026 despite ongoing geopolitical uncertainty, reinforcing its positive outlook for the port operator as stronger exports typically translate into higher container volumes.
The firm also expects volume growth to offset a normalisation in value-added services charges during the second quarter following the resolution of port congestion seen in January.
Management has guided for container volume growth of 3.0 per cent year-on-year in April, compared with an approximately 1.0 per cent decline in the first quarter.
The firm has maintained its full-year throughput growth forecast of 4.5 per cent, in line with management's guidance for low single-digit growth.
Following the revision to its tariff assumptions, RHB Research has raised its earnings forecasts for FY26 to FY28 by between 4.0 per cent and 6.0 per cent.
It said the main downside risks to its outlook include weaker than expected container throughput and higher operating costs.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Four headwinds threaten Malaysia's market recovery in 2H
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's economy is expected to remain on a solid footing in the second half of 2026, underpinned by resilient domestic demand, robust electrical and electronics (E&E) exports and a stable inflation outlook.
However, investors will need to navigate four key headwinds, according to Hong Leong Investment Bank Bhd (HLIB).
The research house said the main risks include lingering global supply chain disruptions, a stronger US dollar, rising political uncertainty ahead of a possible early general election and the proposed expansion of the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI.
The first risk is the persistence of global supply chain disruptions, even if geopolitical tensions in the Middle East begin to ease.
"Taking a leaf from the post-pandemic experience, we note that the Global Supply Chain Pressure Index (GSCPI) stayed elevated for about a year in 2022 despite economic reopening, meaning it takes time for supply chains to normalise.
"Broadly, the FBM KLCI has reacted negatively during periods of heightened GSCPI readings, with an inverse correlation of 25 per cent (arguably weak but still noticeable)," it said in a note recently.
The second risk is a stronger US dollar, underpinned by expectations that the US Federal Reserve will maintain a hawkish monetary policy stance. HLIB said this could temporarily weaken the ringgit and dampen sentiment in the domestic equity market.
It has revised its average USD/RM exchange rate assumption for 2026 to 4.10, while expecting the ringgit to recover to 4.05 against the US dollar by year-end.
Third, HLIB highlighted growing political uncertainty ahead of a possible early 16th General Election (GE16) in the fourth quarter of 2026.
"Given the fragmented political landscape on both sides of the divide, it is plausible that a hung parliament could ensue, akin to GE15. As such, the political risk premium on the local bourse could rise as GE16 draws closer," it said.
The fourth headwind is the proposed expansion of the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI from 30 to 50 constituents, which HLIB believes could create a short-term index overhang as the weightings of existing component stocks are diluted.
Based on its estimates, the influence of current constituents would fall to between 83.7 per cent and 83.9 per cent, with banking and utilities likely to experience the largest reduction in index weightings.
At the same time, the research house said the index expansion would broaden market representation and benefit sectors such as automotive, gaming, real estate investment trusts (REITs), ports and technology, which are expected to gain inclusion in the benchmark index.
The banking and utilities sectors are expected to see the largest reductions in index weightings, it said.
The firm expects headline growth in the second half of 2026 to moderate from first-half levels but has raised its 2026 gross domestic product (GDP) growth forecast to 4.7 per cent year-on-year from 4.5 per cent previously, supported by robust electrical and electronics (E&E) exports.
The revised forecast is slightly above the government's point estimate of 4.5 per cent but remains within its projected range of 4.0 per cent to 5.0 per cent.
HLIB also expects inflation to remain contained at 2.0 per cent in 2026, compared with 1.4 per cent in 2025, despite energy price shocks arising from the Iran war, citing Malaysia's subsidised fuel framework, which now includes diesel.
The firm said sustained loan growth in May suggests domestic demand remains intact despite some weakness in leading loan indicators.
Coupled with stronger-than-expected export momentum and a manageable inflation outlook, HLIB expects Bank Negara Malaysia to maintain the overnight policy rate (OPR) at 2.75 per cent throughout the second half of 2026.
HLIB has also slightly revised its 2026 fiscal deficit forecast to 3.6 per cent of GDP from the government's target of 3.5 per cent, saying the impact of subsidy-related pressures on public finances remains manageable.
Meanwhile, HLIB maintained its KLCI earnings growth forecast at 8.9 per cent for 2026 and 5.1 per cent for 2027, while lowering its year-end FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI target to 1,770 from 1,790 previously.
It said the target is based on a price-to-earnings (P/E) multiple of 15.4 times projected 2026 earnings per share (EPS), adding that record-low foreign shareholding of 18.6 per cent as of May 2026, together with persistent underweight positioning by foreign investors, should provide downside support to the market.
On sector allocation, HLIB upgraded the automotive sector to "Overweight" while downgrading the banking and healthcare sectors to "Neutral".
It said the banking sector was downgraded due to the absence of near-term catalysts amid emerging headwinds facing small and medium enterprises (SMEs).
The tactical adjustment also reflects potential overhang concerns arising from the planned expansion of the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI from 30 to 50 constituents, alongside the possibility of a 10 per cent cap on individual company weightings.
Based on its estimates, the banking sector would experience the largest reduction in index weighting, ranging from 623 basis points to 699 basis points, following the KLCI's expansion.
HLIB said system loan growth remained broadly stable at 5.7 per cent year-on-year in May 2026, supported by stronger business lending momentum, although softer loan applications suggest growth could moderate in the coming months.
The firm said deposit growth improved on the back of stronger current account savings account (CASA) balances and foreign inflows, while asset quality remained manageable despite emerging risks from SMEs and inflationary pressures.
It added that banks' margins have come under renewed pressure amid spread compression and a lower-yield lending mix.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Why is GDP not enough?
Article:
Part 1 - Beyond GDP: Understanding Sabah and Sarawak Through Regional Input-Output Analysis
GDP Explains Growth, Not Economic
KUALA LUMPUR: Structure Malaysia has long relied on standard economic indicators such as Gross Domestic Product (GDP), GDP per capita, investment inflows, and public expenditure to measure regional development. These metrics remain essential for assessing overall performance and enabling comparisons across states and over time. However, they reveal only part of the picture: they show the size and growth rate of an economy but offer limited insight into how wealth is created, how industries interconnect, or whether growth in one region translates into broad economic opportunities elsewhere.
This limitation is especially relevant for Sabah and Sarawak. In 2024, Malaysia's economy grew by 5.1 per cent, reaching approximately RM1.65 trillion. Sarawak contributed around RM148.2 billion and Sabah RM84.3 billion, underscoring East Malaysia's strategic importance to national output. Yet GDP alone cannot explain why states with substantial output often experience divergent outcomes in household income, productivity, and industrial development.
The core issue is that GDP measures the value of production without explaining its structure. It does not show where firms source inputs, how industries link together, how technological capabilities are distributed, or how value spreads through the economy. Nor does it indicate whether growth generates strong multiplier effects or remains concentrated in narrow segments.
Two regions can therefore post similar GDP levels while displaying fundamentally different development patterns.
Sarawak vs Penang: Structural Differences
Sarawak's economy is anchored in natural gas, petroleum, energy, and other resource-based industries. In contrast, Penang has become one of Asia's leading electrical and electronics (E&E) manufacturing hubs, supported by a dense ecosystem of suppliers, precision engineering firms, logistics providers, research institutions, and skilled technical workers.
These structural differences determine how economic benefits spread. A large LNG facility generates billions in output but is highly capital-intensive, employs relatively few workers, and relies heavily on imported specialised technology and equipment. Much of its value accrues via exports, corporate profits, and fiscal revenues.
By comparison, an electronics cluster of similar output value supports hundreds of domestic suppliers, engineering firms, transport operators, testing labs, software companies, and SMEs. Every additional ringgit of production stimulates demand across interconnected industries. The key distinction lies in the density of production linkages, not the value of output itself.
Sarawak's Resource-Income Paradox
This explains one of Sarawak's notable characteristics. The state recorded one of Malaysia's highest GDP per capita figures — approximately RM73,426 in 2024, rising to around RM73,757 in 2025 — well above the national average of RM59,167. These numbers suggest strong prosperity.
The mean household income stands at approximately RM6,947 per month, lower than expected given the GDP per capita. This is not a flaw in GDP measurement but a reflection of what it actually captures: production, not the distribution of income generated by that production. Much of the value from Sarawak's petroleum sector flows through export receipts, corporate earnings, royalties, and government revenues. Without strong domestic supplier networks, technological upgrading, and diversified downstream industries, these do not automatically translate into proportionally higher household incomes.
Economists term this the 'resource-income paradox', common in resource-dependent economies where high output does not guarantee broad-based prosperity.
Sabah's Structural Challenges
Sabah faces a different but related issue. Its economy is concentrated in agriculture (especially palm oil), tourism, and lower value-added services. These sectors are important but typically yield lower labour productivity than advanced manufacturing or knowledge-intensive industries. In 2024, Sabah's agriculture sector contracted by 3.4 per cent and mining/quarrying by 5.0 per cent, highlighting vulnerability to global commodity fluctuations.
These characteristics help explain why income growth in Sabah has historically lagged behind more industrialised states despite abundant natural resources. They also contextualise Malaysia's 2013 minimum wage policy, which initially set different levels for Peninsular Malaysia versus Sabah and Sarawak, implicitly recognising differences in productivity, industrial composition, and enterprise structure.
The Need for Regional Input-Output Analysis
Taken together, the experiences of Sabah and Sarawak show that regional prosperity cannot be fully understood through GDP statistics alone, which measure output but reveal little about multiplier effects, income distribution, or why disparities persist despite similar production levels. The Regional Input-Output (R-I-O) Table addresses this gap by mapping flows of intermediate goods and services between industries and across regions, revealing input sources, output destinations, and how activity in one area stimulates another. This shifts the central policy question from whether Sabah and Sarawak contribute significantly to national GDP — which they clearly do — to whether Peninsular Malaysia, Sabah, and Sarawak function as a single integrated production system or as largely separate structures linked mainly through fiscal and administrative arrangements.
Administrative vs. Technical Integration
From a regional input-output perspective, integration is measured by production relationships rather than constitutional arrangements alone. A well-integrated economy features strong backward and forward linkages, where firms increasingly source intermediate inputs domestically, technologies circulate across regions, and industries reinforce one another through dense networks, allowing growth in one area to generate investment, employment, and innovation elsewhere. Malaysia has achieved a high degree of administrative integration through its common Constitution, single currency, integrated financial system, harmonised regulations, and relatively free movement of goods, services, capital, and labour. However, technical or production integration remains far more limited: interregional flows of intermediate goods and services are modest, and output from Peninsular Malaysia is not utilised in Sabah and Sarawak — and vice versa — to the extent expected in a deeply integrated economy. As a result, Malaysia's production architecture still resembles three partially connected regional systems, and weak production linkages inevitably reduce multiplier effects, as heavy reliance on imported machinery, components, or overseas suppliers causes production expenditure to leak outside the national economy, slowing local supplier development and technological diffusion while concentrating the benefits of growth in narrower segments.
International Lessons and Complementarity
Advanced economies demonstrate that long-term competitiveness rests not merely on producing more but on producing together through dense networks of manufacturers, suppliers, logistics providers, research institutions, and specialised services. These interconnected networks themselves become engines of productivity, innovation, and resilience. Malaysia's goal should therefore be complementarity rather than uniformity: a more integrated national production system that links the distinct strengths of its regions through robust domestic supply chains, allowing technological knowledge, skilled labour, and investment to circulate more freely.
Strategic Implications
Viewed this way, regional input-output analysis becomes a powerful strategic planning tool that identifies weak linkages and fragmented value chains, highlights where targeted policies can generate the largest multiplier effects, and tracks whether Malaysia is becoming a more integrated production economy rather than merely a larger one. More than six decades after the Federation's formation, the greatest regional challenge is no longer constitutional but economic integration: GDP reveals how much is produced, while regional input-output analysis explains how it is produced and whether Peninsular Malaysia, Sabah, and Sarawak are becoming one integrated production system. Stronger technical integration, production linkages, and regional value chains are therefore essential to reduce persistent disparities in productivity, household income, and development, requiring Malaysia's next development stage to shift focus from the distribution of wealth to its creation.
----------------------
*
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Can Johor start a blue wave?
Article:
BARISAN Nasional's victory in the  Johor  election should be viewed as more than just an electoral success.
The result may signal a  political shift that could affect future elections.
Whether this becomes a wider Blue Wave will depend on  governance, party organisation, voter behaviour and how rival coalitions respond to public expectations.
Johor has  historically occupied a unique position in Malaysia's political landscape.
As Umno's birthplace and one of BN's strongest  bases, the state has strong   grassroots networks,  experienced  leaders and deep ties   with Malay voters, while remaining politically competitive in urban areas.
BN's victory  cannot be viewed in isolation. It reflects organisational strength, campaign discipline, candidate choices and BN's ability to present  stability as a political  asset.
The broader political environment also contributed to the outcome.
The Madani  government entered the contest with the advantage of incumbency, while BN retained considerable influence among traditional supporters.
The coalition  arrangement reduced  competition among government-aligned parties and allowed campaign resources to be deployed more efficiently.
Meanwhile, Perikatan Nasional  faced  internal and organisational challenges, including differences  between Pas and Bersatu, organisational adjustments and the difficulty of presenting a  unified political narrative.
These developments  raise questions about other states.
Negri Sembilan and Melaka share several demographic and political characteristics with Johor, including sizeable mixed constituencies, established BN organisational structures and competitive urban-rural electoral dynamics.
Pahang and Perak also have strong BN networks capable of influencing future contests should organisational cohesion be maintained.
In contrast, Selangor and Penang present more complex electoral environments where demographic diversity, urban voting behaviour and different political histories require distinct  approaches rather than assumptions based  on Johor's experience.
Northern states governed by Perikatan Nasional, particularly Kedah, Kelantan and Terengganu, continue to demonstrate different electoral characteristics driven by longstanding ideological preferences and local political culture.
Sabah and Sarawak  remain unique because regional parties and state-specific issues continue to dominate electoral calculations.
Consequently, Johor should not automatically be viewed as a direct predictor for every state.
Rather, it provides an important case study illustrating how disciplined organisation and credible governance messaging can reshape electoral momentum.
Malay-Muslim voter sentiment remains a key factor.
Public concerns surrounding economic opportunities, cost of living, employment, inflation, governance credibility and leadership stability  intersect with traditional identity considerations.
Voters appear to be evaluating not only political narratives but also administrative performance, policy consistency and institutional reliability.
This broader assessment may favour coalitions capable of demonstrating practical delivery rather than rhetorical confrontation alone.
From an economic perspective, political stability continues to matter.
Investors generally respond positively to predictable governance, policy continuity and institutional consistency regardless of electoral competition itself.
Johor's strategic position in manufacturing, logistics, foreign investment and cross-border economic integration  reinforces the importance of stable government administration.
Should BN  consolidate this perception, its electoral momentum could extend beyond symbolic victory into broader confidence regarding governance capacity.
However, caution  is needed. Electoral success in one state does not guarantee identical outcomes elsewhere.
Political momentum depends on continued  policy delivery and public support.
Voters have  demonstrated their willingness to reward performance while also withdrawing support when expectations remain unmet.
The main  lesson from Johor goes beyond election numbers.
It suggests that electoral resilience is increasingly built on organisational credibility, institutional discipline, leadership coherence and practical governance rather than historical loyalty alone.
Competing coalitions will  study Johor's outcome, adapting their  strategies in response.
Whether Johor  becomes the catalyst for a wider Blue Wave   remains uncertain.
However, BN's decisive victory has  altered the  political conversation.
If organisational strength continues to be matched by policy credibility, economic stability, and effective governance, Johor may indeed be remembered as the starting point of a broader electoral domino effect.
If not, the result will remain an important but isolated success.
Ultimately, Malaysian voters, not political narratives, will determine whether Johor's experience evolves into a lasting national political realignment.
The writer is a political and economic analyst
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor election: PH miscalculated, say analysts [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Pakatan Harapan's  failure to tailor its campaign to the Johor election context was a key factor behind its heavy loss, said analysts.
International Islamic University Malaysia  political scientist Assoc Prof Dr Syaza Shukri said PH had fundamentally miscalculated by campaigning on national issues.
"PH's offerings during the campaign didn't excite the people. More than that, PH miscalculated by focusing on national issues. This was a state election, and people want to see if PH could do better. It was not convincing," she said.
Universiti Malaya sociopolitical analyst Prof Dr Awang Azman Awang Pawi said PH's performance pointed to deeper structural problems, including a blurring of its political identity, weakening ties with traditional voters, uneven machinery and an overreliance on central figures.
"PH also struggled to explain how its competition with Barisan Nasional in Johor could be reconciled with their cooperation in the federal government. This tension and confusion attracted attention throughout the election.
"Everything was in place for BN to win big."
DAP secretary-general Anthony Loke said on Saturday PH  respected the voters' decision in the Johor election, adding that the coalition had anticipated a difficult campaign  given BN's strong momentum.
"Johor voters have made their choice. We congratulate BN. Our focus now shifts to the Negri Sembilan election, where the situation is entirely different."
University of Tasmania Asia Institute Prof James Chin said PH's difficulties were due to a deeper erosion of credibility — particularly among urban and Chinese voters who had grown disillusioned with the coalition.
The 16th Johor election saw BN win 48 of the 56 seats  to form the state government once again.
For BN, Umno won 36 seats, MCA eight and MIC four.
PH won eight seats (DAP six, PKR one, Amanah one), while Perikatan Nasional failed to win any seat.
Awang Azman said Pas and Bersatu effectively running as separate entities during the Johor election campaign was a structural failure that could repeat itself in the  Negri Sembilan election.
He said PN's failure to win a single seat was a stark warning that a political coalition could not function by sharing a logo alone while operating separate machinery.
He said Pas was operating a dual-track political strategy — formally aligned with Bersatu in PN while portions of its grassroots sympathised with or cooperated informally with Umno — and that this contradiction had become untenable.
Awang Azman said Pas now faced a choice  three approaches in Negri Sembilan: recommitting to PN, opening the door to an understanding with Umno or contesting more autonomously.
"Without clarity, Pas risks losing the trust of both sides — PN supporters view it as disloyal, while Umno may not necessarily be ready to accept it as an official partner."
Awang Azman said  Johor could yet serve as a shock that forced Pas and Bersatu to reconcile, with a possible division of labour emerging — Pas mobilising the grassroots and Bersatu fielding more moderate candidates.
Chin said the PN coalition did not have any real machinery to work with due to divisions between Bersatu and Pas.
"They imploded before the election," he said, adding that Pas compounded PN's woes by quietly directing its supporters to back BN during the campaign.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US strikes Iran as Gulf states targeted in flareup over Hormuz
Article:
WASHINGTON: The US military on Sunday said it had launched a new wave of attacks against Iran, after renewed fighting over the Strait of Hormuz saw several of Washington's Gulf allies targeted by incoming fire.
The latest salvo by US forces began at 2100 GMT on Sunday, Central Command (CENCTOM) said on X, after earlier announcing approximately 140 strikes the previous night.
The flare-up is the latest to undermine an interim agreement between Washington and Teheran aimed at ending their war, which has caused global economic shockwaves since it began in late February.
Oil prices, which have tumbled since the announcement of the agreement, rose over 3.5 per cent when futures trading opened Monday in Tokyo, with the US benchmark WTI jumping above US$74 a barrel.
Iran reported strikes Sunday evening on two of its southern islands while Kuwait, where Teheran has repeatedly targeted US installations, said border posts and an offshore oil platform had been attacked.
The renewed fighting followed an Iranian attack early Sunday on a commercial ship in the Strait of Hormuz, whose crew was forced to abandon it after it went up in flames.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards said after the incident that "the Strait of Hormuz will be closed until further notice and until the end of American interventions in this region," according to state news agency IRNA.
CENTCOM countered on X that the strait was "open to all vessels seeking to lawfully transit."
It said US forces were "positioned and prepared to ensure" freedom of navigation, adding: "Iran does not control the strait. Traffic is flowing."
Control of the waterway has become key leverage for Iran, with an adviser to the country's supreme leader on Sunday saying it was more important than "dozens of atomic bombs."
Mediators have been trying to salvage a diplomatic solution to ending the war after President Donald Trump this week declared a ceasefire over.
Iran's foreign ministry said the US attacks on Sunday had "caused the return of insecurity in the Strait of Hormuz" and "have rendered futile all efforts" at establishing peace in the region.
- 'HIT THEM VERY HARD' -
On Sunday evening, Iranian state media reported at least 10 "enemy projectiles" hitting Qeshm Island, which sits on the Strait of Hormuz.
It also reported strikes on the island of Farur, to the east of Qeshm in the Gulf, that it said killed a telecommunications worker and wounded two others.
Shortly after, Kuwait said three of its land border posts in the north were damaged in an attack, and that an offshore drilling platform "was targeted by a hostile drone," with one person injured.
Teheran said it had targeted two ships in Hormuz early Sunday, including the one that caught fire.
The American military said it had responded with strikes on about 140 targets, and Iranian media reported explosions in Bandar Abbas, Sirik, Jask and on Qeshm, as well as in Khuzestan province, with one soldier reported killed.
"They were giving up everything, and then all of a sudden two hours after that they hit a ship with a drone," he said.
Iran's response to the US strikes came quickly, with sirens and explosions heard in Qatar, the United Arab Emirates and Bahrain, AFP journalists and local authorities reported.
Kuwait said it was working to intercept an attack, while Jordan said three Iranian missiles fell inside the kingdom.
Iran's Guards said they also hit Oman, which has rarely been targeted.
Muscat summoned the Iranian ambassador and handed him a formal protest – a rare move for the sultanate, which has been attempting to balance competing demands from Washington and Teheran.
The attack came just hours after the country hosted Iran's foreign minister to discuss the Strait of Hormuz.
- 'BLATANT' ATTACK -
Sunday's attack on a Cyprus-flagged container ship in the waterway left one Indian sailor missing, New Delhi said.
Muscat, meanwhile, said it had rescued 23 crew members from a commercial ship.
The crew abandoned ship and were on a lifeboat, British maritime agency UKMTO reported, around 17km east of Oman.
Separate Iranian strikes on ships in Hormuz had triggered fighting earlier this week, along with heated rhetoric.
Iran's supreme leader Mojtaba Khamenei has vowed revenge for the killing of his father and predecessor on the first day of the war, and said Iran had compiled a list of individuals to be targeted.
Trump on Saturday said any attempt to assassinate him would lead the United States to "completely decimate" Iran.
The top diplomat for Pakistan, which has been mediating, called for "de-escalation" on Sunday during a phone call with his Iranian counterpart, Islamabad said.
"Dialogue and diplomacy remain the only viable path to resolving disputes and achieving lasting peace," said Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar.
UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres also called for peace, with his spokesman saying "these attacks must stop."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: BN's blue wave: Johor first, Negri Sembilan next?
Article:
JOHOR BARU: As the dust settled following Barisan Nasional's (BN) landslide victory in the 16th Johor election, one thing became clear: BN had correctly read the mood of the electorate.
Rather than centring its message on political rhetoric or federal-state alignment, BN focused on stability, governance and the continuation of development plans already underway in the state — an approach that ultimately delivered an overwhelming mandate.
The win also offered BN a potential roadmap for future contests, showing that voters could be persuaded by a campaign built around continuity and delivery rather than grand political narratives.
As the coalition looks ahead to the Negri Sembilan  election and other electoral tests, the lessons from Johor are likely to feature prominently in its strategy.
The question now is whether a formula that proved successful in Johor can be replicated elsewhere.
BN and its main rival, Pakatan Harapan, entered the contest with contrasting ideas in their  bids to consolidate support for their candidates.
Led by Johor BN chairman Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi, BN's campaign focused on continuing the implementation of the development agenda, building on the groundwork laid by the state government. It spent less time attacking its rivals unless provoked.
On the other hand, PH, through its manifesto "Johor Untuk Semua" (Johor for All), floated the vision of transforming Johor into an economic powerhouse, describing the state as having the potential to become the "Shenzhen of Southeast Asia" if the state government was better aligned with the federal administration.
The vision, however, failed to resonate with the majority of Johor voters, with BN ultimately winning 48 out of 56 seats, securing a super majority.
[[nid:1487360]]
CHANGING TRENDS
Ilham Centre researcher Mujibu Abd Muis said the polls' outcome showed that Malaysian politics is increasingly moving towards "split ticket voting", where voters make choices based on the level of government being contested rather than solely on loyalty to a particular coalition.
He said it also showed that the state administration's performance outweighed federal political considerations.
"Although BN and PH are partners in the unity government in Putrajaya, voters still gave a strong mandate to BN in the state because they viewed issues such as Johor's development, the menteri besar's leadership and administrative stability as factors that are closer to their daily lives.
"This is consistent with our field findings that Johor voters made decisions based more on the state government's performance rather than national sentiments.
"However, I do not view the outcome as meaning that the relationship with Putrajaya is no longer important.
"What has changed is that voters are now more mature in distinguishing between the roles of the state and federal governments," he said.
If a state government is seen as performing well, they are willing to give it a mandate even if it has a different political alignment from the federal government."
Universiti Malaya's Department of Political Science, Public Administration and Development Studies senior lecturer Dr Mohammad Tawfik Yaakub said the outcome showed that voters did not necessarily view a state government led by a different coalition from Putrajaya as a threat.
He concurred with Mujibu that the outcome did not mean alignment between the state and federal governments was no longer important.
"Instead, voters are placing greater emphasis on stability, the state government's performance, strong leadership and the ability to secure cooperation from Putrajaya without necessarily being under the same coalition," he said.
"Ultimately, the people stand to benefit when they receive both state and federal allocations."
IMPORTANCE OF CAMPAIGN NARRATIVES
Both analysts also said campaign narratives were among the important factors that shaped the outcome of the election, which concluded with a 68.73 per cent voter turnout, involving about 1.87 million voters.
While PH's manifesto was seen as offering a comprehensive plan, including development proposals beyond Johor Baru, such a vision was overshadowed by its leaders' and candidates' focus on national issues such as corruption, reforms and attempts to link the state election with efforts to secure former prime minister Datuk Seri Najib Razak's release.
"These issues may be relevant during a general election, but in a state election, voters are more likely to ask who is capable of addressing their daily concerns such as the cost of living, employment, housing, transportation and local development.
[[nid:1487359]]
"BN, meanwhile, understood the nature of the state election. Its campaign focused on state-level issues such as administrative stability, Onn Hafiz's leadership, Johor's economic development, the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone, the Rapid Transit System and policy continuity.
"The outcome of the state election proves an important principle in voter behaviour: the most effective narrative is not necessarily the biggest issue, but the issue that is closest to the daily lives of the people," Mujibu said.
KICK-OFF POINT FOR BN
Tawfik said the Johor win can boost BN's confidence and bargaining power within the federal government.
BN, he said, now has a basis to argue that its brand and public support are making a comeback, allowing it to demand a bigger role in shaping policies and determining the distribution of power.
"However, winning one state is not enough to prove that BN is ready to lead the federal government.
"BN needs to replicate this performance in Negri Sembilan and other states before it can claim that a 'national wave' is emerging."
Political analyst Professor Datuk Dr Awang Azman Awang Pawi described BN's victory in Johor as the "kick-off point" for the coalition's "Blue Wave".
"While the outcome of the Johor polls does not necessarily reflect the sentiments of voters nationwide, the result does marks the kick-off of the Blue Wave.
"Not just Umno, but MCA and MIC also performed well in the election," he said.
In the state election, MCA won eight of the 15 seats it contested, while MIC won all four seats it contested.
Awang Azman said the next test would be the Negri Sembilan election, as most of the seats won by BN there are marginal constituencies.
"The split between Pas and Bersatu will give BN some advantage and improve its chances of putting up a strong showing in Negri Sembilan," he said.
The Election Commission has fixed this Saturday as nomination day and Aug 1 as polling day for the Negri Sembilan election.
[[nid:1487358]]
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran: Latest US strikes have 'rendered futile' recent diplomacy
Article:
TEHERAN: Iran has condemned the latest wave of US attacks on its territory, saying they had "rendered futile" all the diplomatic efforts of the last few months.
Their statement came soon after the US military announced a fresh wave of strikes against Iran on Sunday, saying it aimed to "continue degrading" Tehran's ability to attack commercial ships in the Strait of Hormuz.
"These barbaric attacks are not only a gross violation of the fundamental principles of the United Nations Charter...but also a serious threat to international peace and security," said an Iranian foreign ministry statement.
They had "rendered futile all efforts of the past few months to reduce tension and establish peace in the West Asian region", the statement added.
"The US regime has also caused the return of insecurity in the Strait of Hormuz and disruption of international commercial shipping by openly interfering in the process of Iran implementing the necessary arrangements in the Strait of Hormuz," said Tehran.
The latest US salvo began at 2100 GMT on Sunday, US Central Command (CENTCOM) said on X, adding that President Donald Trump "has directed the strikes to hold Iranian forces accountable."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] At least 27 dead in Bangkok bar fire: PM
Article:
BANGKOK: A massive fire in a Bangkok bar late Sunday night killed at least 27 people and injured dozens, sending screaming patrons fleeing as flames shot from the entrance, according to officials and video verified by AFP.
An AFP journalist saw several black body bags on the ground outside the Rong Beer Na Lat Phrao bar and restaurant early Monday morning, while dozens of emergency service workers thronged the scene.
"The fire spread very quickly, reaching up to the ceiling. Smoke was likely the main cause of death," Bangkok Governor Chadchart Sittipunt told reporters outside the venue on the outskirts of the Thai capital.
He said 27 people were killed and 63 were hospitalised with injuries, including 22 in critical condition, adding that authorities had launched an investigation.
A number of the victims were found near the fire exit and authorities believe there may have been obstructions blocking it, he said.
"I heard loud screaming from a lot of people inside -- chaos happened," Laotian tourist Kan Kutirat told AFP.
He shared a video on Facebook, verified by AFP, that showed patrons -- several with their shirts ablaze -- fleeing into the street as flames surged out the door.
Kan said he was drinking alone at the bar at around 10pm on Sunday when he noticed smoke rising near the stage.
